coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots-based dual-emission ratiometric fluorescent sensors for fluorescence and visual detection of hypochlorite and Cu2+
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Beibei
Wang, Dinghai
Wang, Minhui
Tang, Yecang
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) with blue emission were synthesized by solvothermal method using hydroquinone and 5-aminoisphthalic acid as precursors. The strong oxidation of ClO− caused the fluorescence quenching of CDs at 405 nm, and synchronously generated a new emission peak at 500 nm. Furthermore, upon the addition of Cu2+ to CDs-ClO− system, the green fluorescence at 500 nm was quenched, while the blue emission at 405 nm remained unchanged, due to the complexation between Cu2+ and the amino group on the CDs surface. Meanwhile, the fluorescence color of system changed from blue to bright green and then to dark blue by sequentially increasing the concentrations of ClO− and Cu2+. The fluorescence signal of F 500/F 405 exhibited a linear relationship with the concentration of ClO− and Cu2+ in a certain range, respectively. Thus, a ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on the obtained CDs were developed to sequentially detect ClO− and Cu2+ with detection limits of 0.40 μM and 0.31 μM, respectively. Additionally, the CDs were mixed with polyvinyl alcohol hydrogel to form test strips, which were successfully used for visual detection of ClO− and Cu2+. Satisfactory results were also obtained in the analysis of ClO− and Cu2+ in actual water samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Dual-emission
Ratiometric fluorescent sensor
Hypochlorite
Copper ion

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Ratiometric fluorescence detection of ClO− and Cu2+
ClO− and Cu2+ analysis in real samples
Preparation of paper-based CDs/PVA hydrogel test strip
Possible mechanism for the detection of ClO− and Cu2+
Application of ClO− and Cu2+ in practical detection
CDs/PVA test strip for visual sensing of ClO− and Cu2+

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image (Inset: HRTEM image) (A), the particle size distribution (B), FT-IR spectra (C), and XPS full-spectrum (D) of CDs.

(A) Fluorescence excitation and emission and UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs. (The inset are photographs under visible light (left) and UV-light (right)); (B) Emission spectra of CDs at different excitation wavelengths.

(A) Fluorescence spectra (Inset: Photographs after adding ClO− and Cu2+ under UV light) and (B) UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs, CDs-ClO−, and CDs-ClO−-Cu2+; The fluorescence lifetime decay of CDs and CDs-ClO− for emission peak located at 405 nm (C) and 500 nm (D).

(A) The fluorescence spectra of CDs under different concentrations of ClO−; (B) Photoshop of CDs-ClO− system under UV lamp (ClO− concentrations range from 0 to 60 μM); (C) Relationship between F 500/F 405 and ClO− concentration; (D) Linear relationship between the ln(F 500/F 405) and the concentration of ClO−; Effects of different ions (E) and molecules (F) mixed with CDs solution on its fluorescence intensity (the concentration of each ion and molecule was 30 µM).

(A) and (B) selectivity of CDs-ClO− (the concentration of each ion and molecule was 20 µM); (C) The fluorescence intensity of CDs-ClO− with different concentrations of Cu2+; (D) Linear relationship between the ln(F 500/F 405) and the concentration of Cu2+.
Photoshop of CDs/PVA strips with different concentrations of ClO− and Cu2+ under sun light and UV light.
(a) Synthesis of CDs and the mechanism of ratiometric fluorescence detection of ClO− and Cu2+; (b) CDs/PVA hydrogel test strip for visual detection of ClO− and Cu2+.
Ratiometric fluorescent sensor detected ClO− in practical water samples.
Ratiometric fluorescent sensor detected Cu2+ in practical water samples.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots-based dual-emission ratiometric fluorescent sensors for fluorescence and visual detection of hypochlorite and Cu2+

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Anhui Normal University, The Key Laboratory of Functional Molecular Solids, Ministry of Education, Anhui Laboratory of Molecule-Based Materials, Wuhu 241000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Materials Science
The Key Laboratory of Functional Molecular Solids

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Anhui Normal University, The Key Laboratory of Functional Molecular Solids, Ministry of Education, Anhui Laboratory of Molecule-Based Materials, Wuhu 241000, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A ratiometric fluorescence sensor base on carbon dots (CDs) was developed to sequentially detect ClO− and Cu2+ with high selectivity and sensitivity. The CDs/PVA test strip was successfully used for visual detection of ClO− and Cu2+.

Carbon dots (CDs) with blue emission were synthesized by solvothermal method using hydroquinone and 5-aminoisphthalic acid as precursors. The strong oxidation of ClO− caused the fluorescence quenching of CDs at 405 nm, and synchronously generated a new emission peak at 500 nm. Furthermore, upon the addition of Cu2+ to CDs-ClO− system, the green fluorescence at 500 nm was quenched, while the blue emission at 405 nm remained unchanged, due to the complexation between Cu2+ and the amino group on the CDs surface. Meanwhile, the fluorescence color of system changed from blue to bright green and then to dark blue by sequentially increasing the concentrations of ClO− and Cu2+. The fluorescence signal of F 500/F 405 exhibited a linear relationship with the concentration of ClO− and Cu2+ in a certain range, respectively. Thus, a ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on the obtained CDs were developed to sequentially detect ClO− and Cu2+ with detection limits of 0.40 μM and 0.31 μM, respectively. Additionally, the CDs were mixed with polyvinyl alcohol hydrogel to form test strips, which were successfully used for visual detection of ClO− and Cu2+. Satisfactory results were also obtained in the analysis of ClO− and Cu2+ in actual water samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Hypochlorite (ClO−), as a highly effective oxidant, is not only widely used for bleaching, sterilizing, and purifying drinking water, but also plays a vital role as a reactive oxygen species in a variety of pathological and physiological processes [1,2]. However, excessive intake of ClO− can pose a serious threat to the health of organisms, leading to some diseases such as arthritis, neuronal degeneration, and even cancer [3–5]. Copper ions (Cu2+) have been used in industrial production, such as smelting and electrical industries, due to their excellent electrical conductivity. However, improper handling of Cu2+ will cause environmental pollution [6]. Moreover, as one of the essential trace elements for human body, Cu2+ deficiency may reduce protein activity, resulting in anemia and cell metabolism disorders, etc. [7], while excessive intake can lead to Alzheimer's disease, Parkinson's disease and other diseases [8,9]. The U.S. Environmental Protection Agency recommended that the appropriate amount of Cu2+ in drinking water is about 20 μM [10]. Over the years, various methods for detecting hypochlorite and Cu2+ have been reported, such as fluorescence spectroscopy [11], electrochemical method [12], atomic absorption spectroscopy [13], inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry [14]. Among them, fluorescence spectroscopy is particularly attractive in sensing applications due to its high sensitivity, rapid response, simple operation, and low cost.

Carbon dots (CDs) have been widely used in chemical sensing due to their unique advantages of high stability, low cytotoxicity, small size, and good dispersion [15–19]. Traditional CD-based fluorescence sensors detect analytes mostly by changing the fluorescence intensity of a single wavelength, which is easy to be interfered by background, external environment and sensor concentration [20,21]. The ratiometric fluorescent sensor designed by the ratio of the fluorescence intensity of two emission bands can reduce the interference of external factors and improve the detection sensitivity [22,23]. Up to now, some new ratiometric fluorescent chemical sensors have been developed by preparing double-emission fluorescent CDs or the combination of two fluorescent substances. Qi et al. [24] prepared a double-emission fluorescent probe by modifying dopamine on the surface of CDs, which was successfully used for ClO− detection. Similarly, Wu et al. [25] constructed a sensitive dual emission sensor for detecting Cu2+ based on silica coated blue carbon dots grafted with red cadmium telluride quantum dots. In the work reported by Zhou et al. [26], a ratiometric fluorescent sensing platform that could respond to ClO− rapidly and precisely was designed by blending the green CDs and red CDs. The preparation of these sensors usually requires complex functionalization or combination with other materials [27–29]. Facile synthesis of CDs-based ratiometric fluorescent sensors has always been desired. Recently, Guo et al. [30] synthesized a double-emission NCDs based on different excitations using a one-step hydrothermal method for simultaneous detection of ClO− and Cu2+. This type of sensor that can accurately detect and analyze two ions with opposite charges at the same time is relatively rare [31]. In addition, when in contact with the analyte, the fluorescence color of ratiometric fluorescent sensors usually undergoes significant changes, making it possible to develop fluorescent test paper for visual detection of analytes. For example, Eu-CDs loaded filter paper developed by Shen’s team was utilized as a sensor for the visual detection of tetracycline [32].

According to previous reports, the phenol hydroxyl group is easily oxidized to a quinone structure, which in turn forms quinone imines with the amino group [33,34]. Inspired by the above research, CDs was prepared by one-step solvothermal method using hydroquinone (HQ) and 5-aminoisophthalic acid as precursors. The obtained CDs was remarkable stability against photobleaching and good salt-tolerance. As shown in Scheme 1 , after the addition of ClO−, the blue fluorescence of CDs was quenched, while a green emission peak was generated at 500 nm. More interestingly, when Cu2+ were introduced into the CDs-ClO− system, the fluorescence intensity of green gradually decreased while that of blue remained almost unchanged. Hence, a ratiometric fluorescent sensor for detecting ClO− and Cu2+ was developed. In addition, the color transformation could be obviously observed under UV lamp as the concentration of ClO− and Cu2+increased, respectively. Thus, a test strip based on CDs and hydrogel was prepared, which was successfully applied to the visual detection of both analytes.

The CDs was prepared by one-step solvothermal process, and the reaction conditions were similar to our previous work [35]. Briefly, HQ (200 mg) and 5-aminoisophthalic acid were completely dissolved in 30 mL ethanol, and then heated in Teflon-lined autoclave at 180 °C for 8 h. The reaction solution was further cooled to room temperature, filtered and dialyzed. The resulting CDs was stored at 4 ℃ for subsequent use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric fluorescence detection of ClO− and Cu2+
ce:section.ce:para: CDs (50 μL) was mixed with a series of ClO− solutions, and then diluted to 1 mL with pH 7.0 PBS buffer. After the mixture was oscillated for 10 min, the fluorescence spectra excited at 320 nm were collected using a Hitachi F4700 spectrofluorometer. To detect Cu2+, different concentrations of Cu2+ were added to CDs-ClO− system. Similar procedures were adopted for other ions to explore the selectivity of the sensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ClO− and Cu2+ analysis in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the feasibility of the developed sensor in real water samples, the recovery rates of ClO− and Cu2+ in tap water of our laboratory and Huajin Lake water of our school were investigated. The water samples were centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 10 min, and then filtered by 0.22 μm membrane. ClO− and Cu2+ of different concentrations were added by standard addition method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of paper-based CDs/PVA hydrogel test strip
ce:section.ce:para: PVA (0.2 g) and 0.04 g borax were added in 10 mL water. The mixture was heated and stirred until PVA was completely dissolved. Then, 10 mL CDs solution was added and mixed thoroughly. After cooling to room temperature and ultrasonic degassing for 30 min, the mixed solution was poured into the surface dish and dried in a vacuum drying oven at 65 ℃ to form a hydrogel film, which was further cut into a circle test strip with a diameter of 1 cm. To visually detect ClO−, the test strip was soaked in various concentrations of ClO−, dried at room temperature for 30 min, and then photographed under UV light at 365 nm. Similarly, Cu2+ solution with different concentrations were added to the test strips containing CDs + ClO− and processed in a similar manner as above.

The morphology and microstructure of CDs were shown in Fig. 1 A. The obtained CDs was nearly spherical and well dispersed. Its particle size ranged from 0.55 to 3.45 nm with an average particle size of 1.99 nm (Fig. 1B). HR-TEM image (Inset of Fig. 1A) illustrated clear lattice streaks with a spacing of about 0.2 nm, similar to the (100) crystal faces of graphite [36]. Meanwhile, the surface function groups of CDs were confirmed by FT-IR and XPS spectra and the results were shown in Fig. 1C and 1D. A wide absorption peak at 3346 cm−1 belonged to the stretching vibration of O − H/N − H, and 2941 cm−1 indicated the presence of C − H bond. 1682 cm−1 and 1513 cm−1 were stretching vibrations of C = O and C = C [37], respectively. The absorption peaks at 1437 cm−1 and 1079 cm−1 could be attributed to stretching vibrations of C − N and C − O − C, respectively. The characteristic peak at 1342 cm−1 belonged to the bending vibration of the phenol hydroxyl group [38]. From the full spectrum of XPS in Fig. 1D, we could distinguish three characteristic peaks of C1s (284.52 eV), N1s (399.46 eV), and O1s (531.91 eV), whose element contents corresponded to 68.99 %, 4.51 %, and 23.39 %, respectively. In Fig. S1A, high-resolution C1s could be convolved as C = C/C − C (284.8 eV), C − N/C − O (286.2 eV), and C = O (288.0 eV). The peaks at 399.2 eV and 401.9 eV in the N1s spectrum (Fig. S1B) belonged to C − N − C and N − H, respectively. The spectra of O1s in Fig. S1C was subdivided into two peaks at 531.18 eV and 532.68 eV, corresponded to C − O and C = O [39], respectively. Both FT-IR and XPS confirmed that the CDs surface contained amine, carboxyl, hydroxyl, and hydroquinone residues. These hydrophilic functional groups not only improved the water solubility of CDs, but also provided active sites for the reaction with ClO−.

The optical properties of CDs were investigated by UV–vis absorption spectrum and fluorescence spectrum. Two absorption peaks (Fig. 2 A) at 290 and 320 nm were attributed to the electron transitions of π-π* for C = C [40] and n-π* for C = O [41], respectively. When CDs was excited with a wavelength of 320 nm, a fluorescence peak at 405 nm appeared. The aqueous solution of CDs was light brown in visible light and showed blue fluorescence under a 365 nm UV lamp (inset of Fig. 2A). In addition, the optimal emission peak was centered at 405 nm under various excitation wavelengths, showing excitation independence, which was attributed to the uniformity of the size and surface state of CDs. The absolute quantum yield of CDs was 6.91 %.

Maintaining optical stability in different environments is one of the important elements for the subsequent application of CDs. The effect of pH value was shown in Fig. S2A. The CDs emitted strong and stable fluorescence in the range of pH 6.0–10.0, but its fluorescence intensity was relatively weak under acid condition. This result was attributed to the protonation of N − H and − COOH on CDs surface [42]. Fig. S2B displayed that even continuously exposed to ultraviolet light for 2000 s, the fluorescence intensity was not significantly disturbed, indicating the CDs exhibited good photobleaching resistance. In addition, the CDs maintained high stability as temperature increased from 25 to 75 °C (Fig. S2C) and the concentration of NaCl in the solution was up to 1.0 M (Fig. S2D). In summary, the prepared CDs had excellent stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible mechanism for the detection of ClO− and Cu2+
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 3 A illustrated that when ClO− was added in CDs solution, the fluorescent intensity at 405 nm decreased, and a new peak at 500 nm emerged clearly. Correspondingly, the fluorescence color changed from blue to green. To get insights into the interaction mechanism, the changes of CDs after the addition of ClO− were characterized by UV–Vis, FT-IR, and fluorescence spectrometer. As shown in Fig. 3B, with the introduction of ClO−, the absorption peak at 290 nm weakened and the absorption band at 320 nm red-shifted. A similar phenomenon was observed for the oxidation of phenolic hydroxyl groups [43]. Fig. 1C illustrated that the characteristic peak belonging to the phenolic hydroxyl group at 1342 cm−1 was weakened, while the peak intensity of C = O at 1780 cm−1 and 1650 cm−1 increased [38]. Compared with CDs, the percentage of C = O in CDs-ClO− system also elevated (Fig. S1D). As previously reported, HQ is easily oxidized to form 1, 4-benzoquinone [33], an excellent electron acceptor. According to these data, we speculated that the remaining hydroquinone groups on the surface of CDs were oxidized to quinone, which accepted electron from the fluorophore, leading to the fluorescence quenching of CDs through electron transfer effects [29]. The fluorescence decay curve in Fig. 3C further supported the above inference. In order to further confirm the oxidation of ClO−, glutathione (GSH), an excellent quinone reduction agent reported in the literature [44], was introduced into the CDs-ClO− system. As shown in Fig. S3A, the addition of GSH restored the fluorescence of the CDs almost to its original position, which indicated that the phenol hydroxyl group on the surface of the CDs was oxidized to quinone groups. Moreover, the benzene ring of quinone has a lower electron density, and the nitrogen atom on CDs surface has a higher electron density and a smaller steric hindrance. Therefore, the resulting 1, 4-benzoquinone may form n-π clathrate and even produce quinone-imine with the amino group [45,46]. These results may be responsible for the generation of new emission at 500 nm. The morphology and particle size of the CDs after the addition of ClO− were shown in Fig. S3C and D. The morphology of CDs was still spherical, while its particle size increased from 1.99 nm to 4.44 nm, which is the result of CDs aggregation caused by the formation of n-π clathrate or quinone-imine [47]. At the same time, Fig. 3D showed that the fluorescence lifetime at 500 nm increased with the increase of ClO−, providing a convincing evidence that new substances were formed in the system [34].

Interestingly, when Cu2+ was added to the CDs-ClO− system, the fluorescence at 500 nm was quenched significantly, while the emission at 405 nm remained almost unchanged (Fig. 3A). From Fig. 3B, it was found that the UV–vis absorption spectrum of CDs-ClO−-Cu2+ was similar to that of the CDs. Moreover, the characteristic peak of the amine group for CDs-ClO−-Cu2+ was weaken (Fig. 1C). These data suggested that the added Cu2+ might be chelated with amine group to form a copper amine complex, which broke the interaction between 1, 4-benzoquinone and amino group as discussed above. As we know that EDTA can selectively bind with copper ions, and the chelating ability of Cu2+ with it is much stronger than that with amino groups [48]. As illustrated in Fig. S3B, the quenched fluorescence of CDs-ClO−-Cu2+ system was recovered after addition of EDTA, indicating Cu2+ ion was released from the copper amine complex [49]. These results clearly confirmed the formation of a complex between Cu2+ and amino group on the CDs surface.

To achieve a better condition for detecting ClO−, the reaction time and pH value were optimized, and the results were displayed in Fig. S4. The optimal reaction time was 10 min and the optimal pH value was 7. Under these conditions, various concentrations of ClO− were added into the CDs dispersion. As shown in Fig. 4 A, the fluorescence at 405 nm gradually weakened with increases the concentration of ClO−. In the meantime, the fluorescence intensity at 500 nm gradually increased. Moreover, the solution color changed from blue to green under UV lamp (Fig. 4B), providing the possibility for visual detection of ClO− using CDs as a ratiometric fluorescence sensor. The relationship between the ratio of F 500/F 405 and the concentration of ClO− was illustrated in Fig. 4C. The logarithm of F 500/F 405 showed satisfactory linearly relationship with ClO− concentration in the ranges of 0 − 28 µM (R 2 = 0.990) and 28 − 65 µM(R 2 = 0.999), respectively (Fig. 4D). The corresponding limit of detections (LOD) were 0.40 µM and 0.53 µM following the equation of LOD = 3σ/k. Compared with fluorescence sensors reported in Table S1 for the assay of ClO−, the ratiometric fluorescence sensor designed in this paper had a lower detection limit and more accurate analysis.

Excellent selectivity is one of the pivotal factors in evaluating the practical application of a sensor. Under the same experimental conditions, we explored the effects of common ions and molecules for detecting ClO− by CDs. It could be distinctly observed from Fig. 4E and 4F that only ClO− cause the fluorescence at 405 nm significantly reduced, and simultaneous produced a new emission. Other interfering substances, such as of MnO4 −, Cr2O7 2−, and H2O2 with oxidizing properties, did not induce similar effects.

As discussed above, the possible mechanism for detecting ClO− was that the phenol hydroxyl group on CDs surface was oxidized and then formed quinone imide with the amine group. Therefore, some common substances, especially some reducing substances, were introduced into the CDs-ClO− (50 µM ClO−) system. As shown in Fig. 5 A and 5B, only Cu2+ induced a significant fluorescence quenching, while other interfering substances showed negligible effect, suggesting that the CDs-ClO− had excellent selectivity for Cu2+. The fluorescence response time towards Cu2+ was only 3 min (Fig. S5). On this basis, the sensitivity of CDs-ClO− for assaying Cu2+ was further studied. Fig. 5C and 5D indicated that the emission peak at 500 nm gradually decreased with the increase of Cu2+. Ln(F 500/F 405) exhibited a good linear relationship with Cu2+ concentration in the range of 0 − 45 µM. The linear equation was ln(F 500/F 405) = 0.5011 − 0.0499[Cu2+], and the calculated LOD was 0.31 µM. By comparison with other sensors in Table S2, our sensor had a lower or comparable detection limit.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of ClO− and Cu2+ in practical detection
CDs/PVA test strip for visual sensing of ClO− and Cu2+
ce:section.ce:para: PVA is a kind of synthetic polymer containing hydrophilic groups, which has low toxicity, good mechanical property, and excellent biodegradability [50,51]. Due to many functional groups on the prepared CDs surface, PVA chains can be combined with them through hydrogen bonding. Thus, for visual application, a hydrogel film based on CDs and PVA hydrogel was prepared by crosslinking with PVA using borax as a crosslinking agent. The CDs were dispersed and solidified into the three-dimensional network structure of the hydrogel, which on the one hand could solve its aggregation, on the other hand, the porous structure of the gel made the analyte easy to diffuse and interact with CDs [52,53]. The hydrogel film was cut into circle test strips to detect analytes. As shown in Fig. S6, the pure PVA hydrogel showed no fluorescence under UV lamp, while the CDs/PVA strip fluoresced brightly blue. The prepared strip was immersed into ClO− solution and then dried at room temperature. It was found from Fig. 6 that the fluorescence color exhibited a noticeable change from blue to green with increase the concentrations of ClO−. Similarly, CDs/PVA-ClO− strip was treated with Cu2+ and exposed to 365 nm UV light. The green fluorescence gradually switched to dark blue in response to increasing concentrations of Cu2+ to 35 μM. These results suggested that CDs/PVA test strip had the potential to be developed for visual detection of ClO− and Cu2+ as a biocompatible and environmentally friendly portable fluorescence sensor. Fluorimetry integrated with smartphones is a fast, convenient, and intuitive new method of target detection [25]. But we had not achieved satisfactory results in trying to construct a real-time quantitative fluorescent sensor for two ions by using the smartphone-assisted strategy. This may be because the fluorescence color change of the CDs is not obvious enough induced by ClO− oxidation, and the quantum yield of the prepared CDs is not high. Smartphone-assisted fluorescence sensors for target analysis are being further designed.

In summary, we successfully fabricated CDs by one-step solvothermal method. The strong oxidation of ClO− not only quenched the fluorescence of CDs at 405 nm, but also induced a new emission at 500 nm. On the other hand, the addition of Cu2+ could effectively quench the fluorescence at 500 nm, which was attributed to form cupric amine complexes. Based on the change of the F 500/F 405, the as-synthesized CDs served as ratiometric fluorescent sensors for the rapid and highly sensitive detection of ClO− and Cu2+. The proposed method was feasible for detecting ClO− and Cu2+ in practical water with rapid response and accurate measurement. Moreover, the prepared CDs/PVA test strip was successfully applied for visual detection of ClO− and Cu2+. Therefore, the sensor developed by us has a potential application prospect in the field of chemical sensing.

Beibei Guo: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Data curation. Dinghai Wang: Validation, Software. Minhui Wang: Investigation, Data curation. Yecang Tang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2024.124516.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

Coord. Chem. Rev.

ACS Sensors

Anal. Bioanal. Chem.

Int. J. Exergy

Pflugers Arch. - Eur. J. Physiol.
